html:
Senior Companion Abu Musa testifies two entire Quranic surahs — one as long as Surah 9 — are now lost Abrogation Scripture Integrity Internal Contradictions Strong Muslim #2303
"We used to recite a surah which we resembled in length and severity to Surah Bara'a [Surah 9, 129 verses] — I have forgotten it with the exception of this which I remember out of it: 'If there were two valleys full of riches, for the son of Adam, he would long for a third valley, and nothing would fill the stomach of the son of Adam but dust.' And we used to recite a surah which we resembled to one of the surahs of Musabbihat, and I have forgotten it..."

What the hadith says

Abu Musa al-Ash'ari — senior Companion, governor of Basra under 'Umar and 'Uthman — addresses 300 of Basra's Quranic reciters and warns them about hardening of the heart. He then discloses two surahs the Companions used to recite that are no longer in the canon: (1) one matching Surah al-Tawbah (Surah 9, 129 verses) in length and severity — Abu Musa preserves only one fragment; (2) one resembling the Musabbihat group (Surahs 57, 59, 61, 62, 64) — Abu Musa preserves only two fragments, one of which partially parallels extant Q 61:2 but the rest is gone. He uses the passive form unsituha — "I have been made to forget it" — invoking the classical Sunni convention for divinely-willed forgetting.

Why this is a problem

  1. A senior Companion publicly confirms the loss of two entire surahs, one the length of Surah 9. Abu Musa is among the highest-authority Companion-narrators. His public statement to 300 Basra reciters is not a private rumour — it is a formal disclosure to an audience of Quranic professionals.
  2. The "made to forget" framing acknowledges the content was divine revelation, now absent. If the surahs were merely abrogated text that was never Quran, Abu Musa would not describe them as Quran the reciters "used to recite." The passive construction concedes divine authorship of material no longer in the mushaf.
  3. This is independent testimony separate from the "stoning verse" / "ten sucklings" / Aisha's goat material. The Quran-incompleteness problem has multiple independent Companion-level attestations in the Sahihayn, of which this is one.

The Muslim response

Classical scholars invoke the doctrine of naskh al-tilawa — abrogation of recitation — under which Allah may remove a Quranic passage from the canon while abrogating its legal or textual force. The "made to forget" formula is read as confirming divine intent: Allah caused these passages to be forgotten as part of a deliberate simplification of the scripture for the community. The Uthmanic mushaf is therefore complete by definition — it contains everything Allah intended to preserve.

Why it fails

The naskh al-tilawa doctrine is a post-hoc theological construct designed to accommodate exactly these reports. It concedes that text genuinely present in earlier Quranic recitation no longer exists — and then reframes the loss as divinely intended. But Q 15:9 promises "We have sent down the Reminder and We will preserve it"; Q 85:21-22 calls it a "preserved tablet." If Allah's preservation plan included deliberate removal of Surah-9-length passages from a text He declared preserved, the promise of preservation is qualified in a way the Quran itself never acknowledges.

Sahih Muslim's seven-day creation sequence directly contradicts the Quran's repeated six-day creation Cosmology Internal Contradictions Scripture Integrity Strong Muslim #6880; contrast with Q 7:54, Q 11:7, Q 50:38
"Abu Hurairah reported that Allah's Messenger took hold of my hands and said: 'Allah, the Exalted and Glorious, created the clay on Saturday and He created the mountains on Sunday and He created the trees on Monday and He created the things entailing labour on Tuesday and created light on Wednesday and He caused the animals to spread on Thursday and created Adam (peace be upon him) after Asr on Friday...'"

Compare: "Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and earth in six days..." (Q 7:54; repeated at Q 10:3, 11:7, 25:59, 32:4, 50:38, 57:4)

What the hadith says

Sahih Muslim preserves a sahih-graded hadith via the Abu Hurairah chain giving a day-by-day creation week: Saturday (clay), Sunday (mountains), Monday (trees), Tuesday (laborious things), Wednesday (light), Thursday (animals), Friday after asr (Adam). The sequence covers seven days. The Quran specifies six days across seven independent verses. The hadith is not preserved in Bukhari, which classical hadith criticism has noted.

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith directly contradicts the Quran on a matter of explicit and repeated divine statement. The Quran uses sittati ayyam (six days) in seven verses; the hadith gives seven named days. This is not a peripheral detail — the six-day creation is among the most repeated cosmological claims in the Quran.
  2. The Saturday-to-Friday week is the Jewish and Christian seven-day creation pattern imported wholesale. The sequence, the day-by-day structure, and the culmination in a named human-creation day are characteristic of Genesis 1 and its Syriac Christian derivative texts circulating in 7th-century Arabia.
  3. The contradiction is unresolved in the canonical tradition. Classical scholars have either declared the hadith weak (despite its Muslim preservation) or attempted strained harmonisations that require redefining what "six days" means in the Quran.

The Muslim response

Some classical scholars declared the hadith's chain problematic despite its Muslim preservation, pointing to the Abu Hurairah chain's dependence on Ka'b al-Ahbar (a Jewish convert) as a potential Israeliyyat contamination. Others argue ayyam (days) in the Quran refers to divine epochs, not calendar days, making the seven-day hadith compatible with a Quranic "six epochs." Modern apologists note that Bukhari omitting the hadith weakens its standing relative to the Quran.

Why it fails

The Ka'b al-Ahbar contamination argument, if valid, raises the question of why Muslim included the hadith as sahih in the first place. The "epochs, not days" reading of the Quran's ayyam is a post-hoc harmonisation applied to remove the contradiction rather than a reading the text itself demands; the same word ayyam appears in the same Quran without any qualifier suggesting metaphorical usage. If one hadith in the second-most-authoritative Sunni collection directly contradicts the Quran on an explicit and repeated claim, the reliability of the collection's grading criteria is a legitimate question.

"The hearts of all the sons of Adam are between two of the fingers of the Compassionate Lord" — Allah physically holds and rotates human hearts at will Theology Internal Contradictions Free Will Strong Muslim #6586; paralleled at Tirmidhi #2140
"Verily, the hearts of all the sons of Adam are between the two fingers out of the fingers of the Compassionate Lord, as one heart. He turns that to any (direction) He likes. Then Allah's Messenger said: 'O Allah, the Turner of the hearts, turn our hearts to Thine obedience.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad teaches that all human hearts — treated as one collective heart — are positioned between two of Allah's fingers, and Allah rotates or directs them however He wills. The Arabic is precise: bayna isba'ayn min asabi' al-Rahman — "between two fingers from the fingers of the Most Merciful" — implying Allah has multiple fingers, of which two hold the totality of human will. Muhammad then prays: "O Turner of the hearts, turn our hearts to Thine obedience" — confirming that hearts are divinely redirectable. The hadith is sahih-graded in both Muslim and Tirmidhi across multiple chains.

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith ascribes literal fingers to Allah in precise anatomical language. The dual isba'ayn picks out exactly two, drawn from a larger set (min asabi'). Classical Sunni theology fractured on whether to read this literally (Athari/Hanbali: Allah has fingers, bila kayf) or figuratively (Ash'ari/Maturidi: "fingers" means controlling power). The hadith's grammar does not naturally support the figurative reading.
  2. If Allah rotates hearts "to any direction He likes," human moral responsibility is incoherent. Muhammad's follow-up prayer — asking Allah to turn hearts toward obedience — acknowledges that Allah does the directing. If hearts are divinely oriented, the theological foundation of accountability, Hell, and reward is destabilised: how is a person responsible for the orientation Allah gave their heart?
  3. The image contradicts Q 42:11 (laysa ka-mithlihi shay' — "there is nothing like Him") by depicting a deity who holds billions of human hearts between two physical fingers like a craftsman holding a tool.

The Muslim response

Athari scholars accept the finger-description with bila kayf ("without asking how") — Allah has fingers appropriate to His divine nature, unlike human fingers. Ash'ari scholars read the fingers as an expression of Allah's attribute of control (qudra) and reject the literal anthropomorphic reading. Both schools argue the hadith is compatible with human accountability because Allah's directing of the heart is through His foreknowledge and decree, not a coercive overriding of human choice.

Why it fails

The bila kayf approach names a solution without providing one — it affirms that Allah has fingers while simultaneously denying that the claim means anything that can be examined. The accountability reconciliation requires a distinction between "Allah directing the heart" and "Allah coercing the heart" that the hadith's own text does not make; the Arabic yusarrifu (to rotate, redirect, modulate) describes active divine causation of heart-orientation, not passive divine permission of free choices. Muhammad's prayer for Allah to direct hearts toward obedience treats the divine heart-rotation as a service to be petitioned — confirming the mechanism is real, not metaphorical.

A man who murdered 100 people is saved because angels measured the ground and found him slightly nearer the piety-land Strange / Obscure Theology Moral Problems Strong Muslim #6835; paralleled at Bukhari #3470
"There was a person before you who had killed ninety-nine persons... He came to [a scholar] and told him that he had killed one hundred persons and asked him whether there was any scope for his repentance to be accepted. He said: Yes; what stands between you and the repentance? You better go to such and such land... So he went away and he had hardly covered half the distance when death came to him and there was a dispute between the angels of mercy and the angels of punishment... He said: You measure the land to which he has drawn near. They measured it and found him nearer to the land where he intended to go, and so the angels of mercy took possession of it."

What the hadith says

A man who killed 99 people seeks religious counsel. A monk tells him repentance is closed; the man kills the monk (100 victims). A scholar tells him repentance is open but he must relocate to a pious community. The man sets out and dies halfway. Angels of mercy and punishment dispute his soul; an arbitrating angel proposes a geographic measurement. The measurement finds him marginally closer to the destination. The angels of mercy claim him. Some parallel chains add that Allah miraculously contracted the destination-land to ensure the mercy-outcome.

Why this is a problem

  1. Salvation turns on physical geography rather than moral transformation. The man performed no repentance act, compensated no victim, and expressed no genuine contrition recorded in the text. He died having covered approximately half a journey. The determining factor is not the state of his heart but the angular measurement of his corpse's proximity to a geographic destination.
  2. The mechanism is spatial-mechanical magic. Distance-measured salvation is the structure of sympathetic magic, not theology. The "water of life" variant in related chains compounds the magical register with a botanical-resurrection element.
  3. The monk is punished for providing accurate theology. Under classical Sunni doctrine — repentance is open to all — the monk gave the wrong answer and died for it. The hadith canonises a story in which a murder victim's death is his own fault for incorrect theological advice.

The Muslim response

Apologists emphasise the hadith's central teaching: divine mercy is infinite and available even to the worst sinners, provided they genuinely intend to reform. The geographic measurement is understood as Allah's way of honouring sincere intention — the man's direction of travel demonstrated his will to change. Allah's miraculous contraction of the distance in some variants shows that He actively assists those who turn toward Him. The monk's death is understood as a consequence of his having put himself in a murderer's path without being equipped to counsel him.

Why it fails

If the lesson is divine mercy for sincere repentance, the geographic mechanism is theologically superfluous — Allah could simply apply mercy to sincere intent without angel-mediated land-measurement. The fact that measurement is the determinative act means that had the man died one step closer to his origin-land, punishment-angels would have prevailed, regardless of identical intention. A salvation-doctrine that treats a man's location at the moment of death as the controlling variable in his eternal fate is not a mercy theology — it is a spatial lottery wrapped in a mercy lesson.

Muhammad wept at his mother's grave: Allah refused his request to seek forgiveness for her Prophetic Character Theology Moral Problems Strong Muslim #2144; paralleled at Abu Dawud #3234
"The Apostle of Allah visited the grave of his mother and he wept, and moved others around him to tears, and said: 'I sought permission from my Lord to beg forgiveness for her but it was not granted to me, and I sought permission to visit her grave and it was granted to me, so visit the graves, for that makes you mindful of death.'"

What the hadith says

Muhammad visits the grave of his mother Aminah bint Wahb (died c. 576–577 CE, when Muhammad was about six years old). He weeps openly. He reports two permissions sought from Allah: grave-visitation (granted) and forgiveness-intercession (refused). The canonical Sunni implication is that Aminah died as a Meccan polytheist, falls under the unforgivable-shirk rule of Q 4:48/4:116, and is in Hell. Even the Prophet's intercession cannot retrieve her.

Why this is a problem

  1. The canonical Sunni reading places Muhammad's own mother in Hell. Aminah died approximately 33 years before Muhammad's prophetic call; she had no opportunity to profess Islam. Classical Sunni eschatology places polytheists who die without hearing the message in Hell regardless. The hadith's "permission refused" is the doctrinal seal on that outcome.
  2. Q 17:15 states "We never punish until We have sent a messenger." Aminah lived and died before Islam was preached. The Quranic principle that pre-prophetic populations are not held accountable should protect her. The hadith produces a direct conflict between this Quranic promise and the implied outcome of the forgiveness refusal.
  3. It undercuts the Prophet's special intercessory status. Muhammad is described throughout the Sunna as the possessor of the supreme intercession (al-maqam al-mahmud). That he cannot intercede for his own mother — who died before he was a prophet — makes the scope of prophetic intercession harder to define coherently.

The Muslim response

Apologists offer several framings: some classical scholars argued Aminah's father's household had received enough of Abraham's monotheistic legacy to constitute a prior "message," placing her outside Q 17:15's protection. Others argue Allah's refusal of forgiveness-permission demonstrates the absolute rule of divine justice — even prophethood does not create nepotistic exceptions. A minority tradition holds that the grave-weeping hadith records Muhammad's grief over a theological reality, not a divine verdict, and that Aminah's final state is unknown to humans.

Why it fails

The "Abraham's legacy" argument is an ad hoc construction created specifically to exempt Aminah from Q 17:15 without textual support. If pre-Islamic Mecca counted as having received a prior message sufficient to nullify Q 17:15's protection, the verse's protection applies to no one in Arabia — which vitiates the principle entirely. The "unknown final state" reading requires dismissing the hadith's plain content: Muhammad explicitly said permission to seek forgiveness was refused, which the Sunni tradition consistently reads as an indication of the outcome, not a neutral unknowable.

Muhammad prayed over the chief hypocrite's funeral against Umar's objection — then Q 9:84 was revealed prohibiting it Prophetic Character Convenient Revelation Internal Contradictions Strong Muslim #6853; Bukhari #1366, #4670; Q 9:84
"When Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul died... 'Umar stood up and caught hold of the garment of Allah's Messenger and said: 'Allah's Messenger, are you going to conduct prayer for him though Allah has forbidden you to offer prayer for him?' Thereupon Allah's Messenger said: 'Allah has given me liberty (to choose)...' Umar said: 'But he is a hypocrite.' Allah's Messenger nevertheless conducted prayer for him, and after this, Allah revealed the words: 'And never pray you for any one of them who dies, nor stand at his grave...' (Q 9:84)."

What the hadith says

Abdullah ibn Ubayy — the canonical "leader of the hypocrites" — died in 9 AH. His sincere Muslim son requested Muhammad's shirt as a shroud and Muhammad's leadership of the funeral prayer. Muhammad agreed to both. Umar physically grabbed the Prophet's garment in protest, citing an existing prohibition he believed applied. Muhammad overruled him by citing Q 9:80 as permitting unlimited supplication. Muhammad prayed the funeral. After the prayer, Q 9:84 was revealed: "Never pray for any of them who dies, nor stand at his grave." Muhammad subsequently confessed he would have sought forgiveness more than seventy times if that could have saved Ibn Ubayy.

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad misread his own revelation and was corrected by post-hoc divine intervention. His interpretation of Q 9:80 as permitting unlimited petitioning was wrong; Allah's correction via Q 9:84 came after the prayer was complete. The canonical record preserves Muhammad making a hermeneutical error on his own scripture.
  2. The revelation arrived after the action, not before — following the pattern of "convenient revelation" throughout the Sira. Umar's objection anticipated the correct ruling; Muhammad overruled it; revelation then validated Umar's original position. The structural pattern raises the question of whether revelation was guiding behaviour or ratifying outcomes.
  3. Muhammad gave his shirt to shroud the man described in the Quran and Sira as Islam's foremost internal enemy. This act of personal honour — followed by the funeral prayer — is then prohibited by divine command. The sequence reveals either a Prophet whose pastoral compassion ran ahead of divine law, or a revelation system that corrected mistakes after they occurred.

The Muslim response

Apologists emphasise Muhammad's pastoral motivation: Ibn Ubayy's sincere Muslim son made a heartfelt request, and Muhammad honoured it out of compassion for a believing family. Q 9:80's language genuinely was ambiguous; Muhammad chose the more merciful reading. The post-prayer revelation clarified the rule definitively, and Muhammad accepted and transmitted it without protest — demonstrating the self-correcting integrity of prophetic revelation. Umar's physical intervention is read as a Companion's legitimate expression of strong concern, not insubordination.

Why it fails

Q 9:80's text — "even if you seek forgiveness seventy times, Allah will not forgive them" — is not genuinely ambiguous about the direction of the ruling. Umar read it correctly; Muhammad did not. If prophetic revelation is meant to provide unambiguous divine guidance, a reading error by the Prophet himself on a text he received directly undermines the premise. The "self-correcting system" framing reframes the problem as a feature — but a correction mechanism that operates after an action has already been completed is not guidance; it is damage control.

Muhammad hears grave-torment, plants fresh palm twigs: "perhaps their punishment may be mitigated as long as these twigs remain fresh" Strange / Obscure Theology Magic / Occult Strong Muslim #582; paralleled at Bukhari #216, #1361
"The Messenger of Allah happened to pass by two graves and said: They (their occupants) are being tormented, but they are not tormented for a grievous sin. One of them carried tales and the other did not keep himself safe from being defiled by urine. He then called for a fresh twig and split it into two parts, and planted them on each grave and then said: Perhaps, their punishment may be mitigated as long as these twigs remain fresh."

What the hadith says

Muhammad passes two graves and supernaturally perceives both occupants being tormented: one for tale-bearing (namimah), one for poor urine-hygiene. Both offenses are classified as minor. Muhammad calls for a fresh palm-twig, splits it, plants one half on each grave, and announces a precise mechanism: torment is mitigated for the duration the twigs remain fresh. Jabir's parallel (#7326) adds that Muhammad miraculously commanded trees to approach before harvesting the twigs.

Why this is a problem

  1. The torment-mitigation mechanism is sympathetic magic, not theology. The operative phrase — "as long as these twigs remain fresh" — ties post-mortem torment-relief to the biological state of vegetation above the grave. This is the textbook structure of late-antique sympathetic magic: a vital-force object (green, living) is believed to influence a supernatural state through physical correspondence. The canonical record preserves the mechanism explicitly, not metaphorically.
  2. The mechanism makes graveyard maintenance metaphysically consequential. If torment-mitigation lasts only while the twigs are green, replacing dried twigs with fresh ones would technically extend the relief. This is the operational logic behind graveyard practices across Islamic cultures — placing fresh palm fronds, basil, or leaves on graves — a practice Salafi reformers condemn as innovation but which has unambiguous canonical basis in this hadith.
  3. The torment is disproportionate to the offenses. Active grave-torment for gossip and urine-droplet carelessness — classified as non-grievous sins — establishes a post-mortem justice system operating on minor infractions with real physical suffering.

The Muslim response

Apologists argue the twig's effect operates through the twig's continuous act of divine glorification (tasbih) — living things glorify Allah, and their glorification is intercessory for the grave's occupant. The duration is tied to the twig's continued life as a glorifying entity, not to magical vital-force. The hadith establishes that living things' glorification can bring spiritual benefit to the dead, which is consistent with broader Islamic doctrine about the spiritual efficacy of dhikr.

Why it fails

If the mechanism is tasbih-intercession by a living plant, the mitigation should continue indefinitely as long as any glorifying entity exists near the grave — which would mean any living organism (grass, insects, trees) should provide the same mitigation without requiring Muhammad's specific twig-planting act. The hadith's framing is precise: Muhammad split one twig into two halves, one per grave, in a deliberate apportioning act. The specificity of the splitting and the freshness-duration condition points to a one-to-one physical correspondence between twig and grave-occupant, not general ambient glorification. The tasbih interpretation is a theologically tidy reframing of what the text actually describes.

"Allah would come to them in a form other than His own Form" — two-form Resurrection theology, Sa'dan-thorn Hell hooks, and prostration-marks surviving Hellfire Theology Internal Contradictions Strange / Obscure Strong Muslim #356; paralleled at Bukhari #4581, #6573
"Verily you would see Him like this (as you see the sun and the moon)… Allah would then come to them in a form other than His own Form, recognisable to them, and would say: I am your Lord. They would say: We take refuge with Allah from thee… Subsequently Allah would come to them in His own Form, recognisable to them, and say: I am your Lord. They would say: Thou art our Lord…"

What the hadith says

Abu Hurairah narrates a long sahih-graded eschatological scene. On the Day of Resurrection, Allah first approaches believers in a form they do not recognise — they refuse Him, saying "we take refuge with Allah from thee." Allah then comes in His own Form, which they do recognise, and they accept. The scene continues: a bridge over Hell (al-Sirat) fitted with Sa'dan-thorn hooks seizes sinners; damned Muslims are rescued by their prostration-marks, which the Fire cannot consume; they are revived with "water of life" and re-grown like seeds in flood-silt. The final vignette shows the last man admitted to Paradise repeatedly bargaining with Allah, who eventually laughs and gives him multiples of what he requested.

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith explicitly states Allah has two Forms — one "other than His own," one His own. The Arabic fi surah ghayri suratihi — "in a form other than His Form" — is not ambiguous. It assumes (a) Allah has a Form (surah); (b) He has multiple Forms; (c) believers have prior visual knowledge of which one is "His own." This is high-order anthropomorphism that cannot easily be harmonised with Q 42:11's "there is nothing like Him."
  2. "Recognising Allah" presupposes prior visual encounter. The believers reject the first Form because they don't recognise it and accept the second because they do. This knowledge of what Allah looks like in "His own Form" is unexplained and theologically inexplicable in a tradition that rejects all visual representations of Allah.
  3. The Sa'dan-thorn Hell-hooks and prostration-mark survival are folk-cosmological elements — a physical mechanism for sorting sinners in Hell based on bodily marks, grafted onto theological doctrine about salvation and punishment.

The Muslim response

Athari scholars accept the two-form description with bila kayf — Allah manifests in forms appropriate to His divine nature that humans can or cannot recognise, without this implying physical limitation or multiplicity in the human sense. Ash'ari scholars read "form" as a mode of manifestation or a relational presentation, not a literal divine body-shape. The prostration-mark survival is understood as a divine miracle preserving the signs of worship. The entire scene is held to be eschatological — operating under different physical laws than the present world.

Why it fails

The bila kayf response applied to the two-form problem produces an incoherent statement: Allah has two Forms, but neither Form tells us anything about what "Form" means in Allah's case. The believers' rejection of the first Form and acceptance of the second requires that they have meaningful prior knowledge of what Allah looks like — a condition that "without asking how" cannot explain away. The "eschatological different laws" response applies equally to any physical claim made in any hadith and functions as a universal defeater that removes the possibility of ever challenging physical descriptions of divine or afterlife realities.

Muhammad sends Abu Hurairah to promise Paradise to shahada-bearers; Umar physically stops him; Muhammad rescinds the teaching Prophetic Character Internal Contradictions Theology Strong Muslim #54, #55
"He gave me his sandals and said: 'Take away these sandals of mine, and when you meet anyone outside this garden who testifies that there is no god but Allah, being assured of it in his heart, gladden him by announcing that he shall go to Paradise.' … 'Umar struck me on the breast and I fell on my back... 'Umar said: Please do it not, for I am afraid that people will trust in it alone; let them go on doing (good) deeds. The Messenger of Allah said: Well, let them."

What the hadith says

Muhammad hands Abu Hurairah his sandals as a token of authority and sends him with a public mission: promise Paradise to anyone who sincerely testifies the shahada. Abu Hurairah leaves and immediately encounters Umar ibn al-Khattab, who on hearing the message strikes Abu Hurairah in the chest hard enough to knock him down, then orders him to return to the Prophet. Umar's argument: people will rely on the shahada alone and stop performing good deeds. Muhammad accepts the reasoning and rescinds the mission: "Well, let them." A parallel incident with Mu'adh ibn Jabal (#55) ends with Mu'adh suppressing the teaching his entire life, disclosing it only on his deathbed.

Why this is a problem

  1. A direct prophetic teaching is overruled by a subordinate's policy objection. If "sincere shahada guarantees Paradise" is theologically true, it is true regardless of social consequences. Muhammad accepts Umar's pragmatic objection — not a doctrinal correction, but a crowd-management concern — and retracts the teaching. The result is a Prophet who calibrates divine truth to congregational behaviour.
  2. Umar uses physical violence against a Prophet-delegated messenger and faces no rebuke. Abu Hurairah is on a direct prophetic commission. Umar — without authorisation — strikes him to the ground and countermands the order. Muhammad accepts the outcome without censuring Umar, without asking if Abu Hurairah is hurt, and without reaffirming his own instruction. The canonical model is that a senior Companion may physically override a direct prophetic command.
  3. The canon preserves both the doctrine and its suppression with no resolution. Sahih Muslim itself retains the shahada-alone-saves tradition in multiple places (#56, Bukhari #99, #128) alongside this incident of its deliberate suppression. The collection offers no jurisprudential rule for how to adjudicate between them.

The Muslim response

Apologists argue Muhammad was exercising pastoral wisdom: the teaching was true but its unqualified broadcast risked producing antinomianism — a community that abandons good works on the assumption that shahada alone is sufficient. Umar correctly identified the risk; Muhammad adjusted the dissemination strategy, not the doctrine. Mu'adh's deathbed disclosure confirms the teaching survived in private transmission. The physical strike is read as an expression of the urgency of Umar's concern, not authorised violence against prophetic authority.

Why it fails

If a true divine teaching cannot be publicly broadcast because it will produce wrong behaviour, the teaching's truth is operationally conditional on audience management — which is not how revelation is presented anywhere else in the Quran or Sunna. The "adjusted dissemination" framing concedes that Muhammad was willing to let people believe something less than the full truth for policy reasons — a model of prophetic communication that fundamentally undermines the reliability of everything else Muhammad publicly taught. Mu'adh sitting on a theological truth his entire life "to avoid sinning by withholding it" is not a satisfying resolution; it is a record of deliberate concealment of canonical doctrine.

A Jew kills an Ansari girl for her jewellery — head crushed between two stones Hudud Prophetic Character Governance Gross / Vile Strong Muslim #4233
"Anas reported that a Jew killed a girl of the Ansar for her ornaments and then threw her in a well and smashed her head with a stone. He was caught and brought to the Messenger of Allah, and he commanded that he should be stoned to death. So he was stoned until he died." Parallel chain (#4232): "He commanded to crush his head between two stones."

What the hadith says

A Jewish man in Medina kills an Ansari girl for her jewellery and crushes her head. Muhammad orders mirror-punishment: the killer is stoned to death, with a parallel chain specifying the head-crushing-between-two-stones method that replicates the original crime.

Why this is a problem

  1. Stoning applied to homicide, not just adultery. Classical jurisprudence knows stoning as the hadd for married adulterers. This hadith extends it to qisas murder — equal retaliation executed by reproducing the killing method. That extension sacralises mirror-punishment as Prophetic juridical precedent.
  2. "Crush his head between two stones" is reproduction-killing. The killer's own technique is applied to him. The execution is calibrated for symbolic precision — theatrical justice, not abstract penalty. Maliki and Shafi'i schools cite this hadith to permit mirror-mode retaliation in homicide cases.
  3. The religious identifiers are load-bearing. The killer is Jewish; the victim is Ansari (Muslim). The hadith retains these markers, embedding inter-religious framing in the judicial precedent regardless of whether both parties being Muslim would change the ruling.
  4. The operational record is current. Modern Saudi and Iranian penal codes permit qisas-by-method for some homicide cases on the basis of this jurisprudential tradition. The hadith is not historical curiosity; it is functioning law.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames this as qisas in operation: the killer's own cruelty rebounds on him, demonstrating the Quranic principle of "a life for a life" (Q 2:178). Modern Muslim teaching adds that qisas permits the victim's family to choose retribution, blood-money, or pardon — making mirror-mode retaliation one option among several, not a general protocol.

Why it fails

The "one specific case" reading is contradicted by the operational record: classical Maliki and Shafi'i jurisprudence generalised the mirror-punishment principle into legal doctrine, and modern penal codes implement qisas-by-method as available retaliation. The "victim-family consent" qualifier is also double-edged: in honour-and-tribal-pressure societies, family "consent" is socially compelled, not freely given. The hadith encodes a juridical structure that operates on the same community pressure governing 7th-century Medina — and the modern tradition has not built adequate procedural safeguards against that pressure.

The Dajjal kills a believer, resurrects him, then cannot kill him again Eschatology Strange / Obscure Cosmology Prophetic Character Strong Muslim #7191
"The Dajjal would say: 'What is your opinion if I kill this person, then I bring him back to life; even then will you harbour doubt in this matter?' They would say: No. He would then kill the man and then bring him back to life. When he would bring that person to life, the man would say: 'By Allah, I had no better proof of the fact that you are a Dajjal than at the present time.' The Dajjal would then make an attempt to kill him again but he would not be able to do that. Abu Ishaq reported that it was said: That person would be Khidr."

What the hadith says

At the end of time, the Dajjal performs a public miracle: he kills a believer and resurrects him. The resurrected man declares his certainty about the Dajjal's identity has increased. The Dajjal attempts a second killing but Allah does not permit it. A later narrator identifies the believer as Khidr.

Why this is a problem

  1. The Dajjal genuinely resurrects the dead. Resurrection is a divine prerogative in standard monotheism. The hadith concedes this prerogative to the Dajjal — or at least a functionally indistinguishable version of it. Modern apologetics claiming the Dajjal only produces the "illusion" of resurrection cannot easily accommodate the text's plain narrative: the man dies, the man returns to life.
  2. The believer's certainty increases because of the miracle. The hadith's punchline produces strange epistemology: the antichrist performing the prophesied feat is what confirms the believer's faith. A tradition's authenticity is supported by a deceiver successfully doing what the prophecy said he would do.
  3. The Khidr identification is post-textual. The hadith says only "a man who would be the best of men." The Khidr gloss is appended — "Abu Ishaq reported that it was said" — a later commentary chain, not the original isnad. Yet classical Sunni eschatology has run on this identification for centuries.
  4. It pre-conditions believers toward charismatic miracle-claimants. A Muslim raised on this hadith expects the false messiah to perform real-looking miracles. In practice, multiple Mahdi-claimants in Islamic history gathered followers by performing spectacular feats. The "true believers reject the miracle" framing is the theory; the historical record is that audiences often follow the miracle-worker.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames the Dajjal's "resurrection" as deception (khida') — an illusion that mimics resurrection to test believers. The believer's faith defeats the deception. Modern teaching often treats the Khidr identification as a folkloric overlay rather than substantive eschatology, and emphasises the geographic specificity (Medina's protection) as divine mercy.

Why it fails

The "deception not resurrection" reading requires the believer's epistemology to be unstable: he has just witnessed what looked like death and return-to-life, and his response is increased certainty that the performer is the Dajjal. If the resurrection were obvious illusion, certainty would not increase. The hadith preserves the certainty-increase precisely because the audience is meant to take the resurrection as a real event whose performer is the antichrist — not as an obvious trick. The "folkloric Khidr" downgrade is also selective: al-Nawawi's Sharh Sahih Muslim and Ibn Hajar's commentaries treat the Khidr identification as substantive, not decorative.

Aisha married at six, sexually consummated at nine — Sahih Muslim confirms it Women Prophetic Character Sexual Misconduct Strong Muslim 3356
"'A'isha reported: Allah's Messenger (may peace be upon him) married me when I was six years old, and I was admitted to his house at the age of nine... Allah's Messenger came there in the morning, and I was entrusted to him." (3309)
"'A'isha reported that Allah's Apostle married her when she was seven years old, and he was taken to his house as a bride when she was nine, and her dolls were with her..." (3311)

What the hadith says

Three separate narrations on Aisha's own authority, preserved in the second-most authoritative Sunni hadith collection. Muhammad married her at six (or seven), consummated the marriage when she was nine, and she still had her dolls with her at that age. Muhammad was in his early fifties.

Why this is a problem

The key addition over the Bukhari entry: Muslim's version makes the child status more explicit by mentioning the dolls. Classical scholarship on the permissibility of playing with dolls partly rests on these very hadiths, because Aisha is depicted as playing with them after her marriage.

The presence of the same report in both Bukhari and Muslim — the two Sahihayn — makes modern revisionist claims that Aisha was actually 18 or 19 structurally untenable. To reject this hadith requires rejecting the entire hadith science apparatus that sustains Sunni Islam. An authentication system whose strongest-possible attestation lands on a nine-year-old's consummation is not vindicated by being robust; it is indicted by it.

The Muslim response

Same rebuttals apply here as for Bukhari — maturity standards in 7th-century Arabia, betrothal norms, Aisha's later agency and authority. With even greater force here because of the duplicate attestation in both Sahihayn, narrated by the woman herself through independent chains.

Why it fails

The apologist's own framing — if this can't be trusted, nothing can — is precisely the point. The critic is not asking Muslims to distrust hadith science arbitrarily; the critic is showing that hadith science, applied consistently, produces a picture contemporary ethics rejects. A transmission system whose strongest-possible attestation lands on a nine-year-old's consummation is not vindicated by being robust; it is indicted by it. The dilemma — revise the ethics or revise the method — is the apologist's to resolve, and every available move makes the classical doctrine of preserved Sunna less defensible.
